questions.question_text: Colorado requires sellers to complete a property disclosure form by statute. True or false?
questions.correct_answer: False — it is voluntary, not mandatory
distractors: True — but only for homes over $500K
True — but only for homes built before 1978
questions.explanation: Colorado has no general statute mandating a comprehensive seller disclosure form — the SPD is a Commission form delivered under the contract. But specific disclosures ARE statutorily required in residential sales: radon (C.R.S. 38-35.7-112), source of potable water (38-35.7-104), special taxing districts (38-35.7-101), and methamphetamine (38-35.7-103), plus federal lead-based paint. Under the standard contract the seller also agrees to deliver a completed SPD.

questions.question_text: Under the current Commission-approved Contract to Buy and Sell (Residential), the seller's obligation regarding the SPD is to:
questions.correct_answer: Deliver a completed, current SPD form by the Seller's Property Disclosure Deadline
distractors: Check a contract box electing not to disclose
Provide it only if the buyer requests it in writing
Have the listing broker complete the form for the seller
questions.explanation: CBS1 section 10.1 obligates the seller to deliver the most current Commission SPD form, completed to the seller's actual knowledge, by the Seller's Property Disclosure Deadline. Section 10.2 separately requires the seller to disclose in writing all adverse material facts actually known, including those discovered after contracting.

questions.question_text: The Commission-approved Seller's Property Disclosure form is used:
questions.correct_answer: As part of the contract, if seller agrees
distractors: Only for properties over 10 years old
Only when the buyer requests it
questions.explanation: The SPD is not a standalone statutory mandate; it operates through the contract. Under CBS1 section 10.1 the seller agrees to deliver a completed SPD by the Seller's Property Disclosure Deadline. Parties could negotiate this away only by modifying the deadline or terms.

questions.question_text: A seller completes the SPD but intentionally omits a known plumbing defect. The seller may face:
distractors: No consequences since SPD is voluntary
questions.explanation: Even though the SPD is voluntary, once completed it must be honest. Intentionally omitting known defects constitutes misrepresentation and can lead to civil liability.

questions.question_text: Which statement about the Colorado SPD is correct?
questions.correct_answer: It is a Commission form, not mandated by state statute
distractors: It is required for all property transfers
It replaces the broker's disclosure duty
Only brokers can complete the form
questions.explanation: No Colorado statute requires the comprehensive SPD, but the standard contract (CBS1 section 10.1) obligates the seller to deliver it, and specific disclosures (radon, water source, special districts, meth) are statutorily required. The SPD never replaces the broker's independent duty to disclose known adverse material facts.

questions.question_text: A broker knows about foundation cracks in a listed property. The seller chose 'not to disclose.' The broker must:
questions.correct_answer: Still disclose the known material fact
distractors: Wait for the buyer to ask about it
Only disclose if the buyer hires an inspector
questions.explanation: Brokers have an independent duty to disclose known adverse material facts regardless of whether the seller provides an SPD. The seller's choice does not eliminate the broker's obligation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is an adverse material fact that a broker must disclose?
questions.correct_answer: A known boundary dispute with a neighbor
distractors: A death that occurred in the property
questions.explanation: Boundary disputes can affect property value and desirability, making them adverse material facts. The seller's moving reasons, property deaths, and purchase price are generally not material facts.

questions.question_text: A broker fails to disclose known water damage to a buyer. The broker may face:
distractors: No penalty if the seller did not disclose
Liability only if the buyer suffered injury
questions.explanation: Failure to disclose known material facts is grounds for both Commission discipline (suspension, revocation, fines) and civil liability to the harmed party.

questions.question_text: Material facts that a Colorado broker must disclose include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Material facts relate to the property's condition, value, or desirability — not the seller's personal financial situation. Structural defects, environmental hazards, and zoning violations are all material facts.

questions.question_text: A transaction-broker learns of a zoning violation on the property. This information must be disclosed to:
questions.explanation: C.R.S. 12-10-407(2) requires a transaction-broker to disclose known adverse material facts to prospective buyers (title, physical condition, defects, environmental hazards) AND to prospective sellers — both disclosure clauses are 'including but not limited to' their examples. A zoning violation affects value and must be disclosed; that it appears in public records does not excuse the duty.

questions.question_text: A property is served by a private well rather than municipal water. The seller must provide:
questions.explanation: C.R.S. 38-35.7-104 requires every residential sale to disclose the source of potable water (well, water provider, or other source) in the contract, SPD, or Source of Water Addendum. For a well, the seller must also provide a copy of the current well permit if available.

questions.question_text: Under C.R.S. 38-35.7-104, if a property's water source is a well, the seller must also provide:
questions.correct_answer: A copy of the current well permit, if one is available
distractors: An estimate of annual water costs
questions.explanation: The statute requires every residential sale contract to disclose the water source — a well, a water provider (with contact details), or another described source — plus an advisory that some providers rely on nonrenewable groundwater. If the source is a well, the seller must also provide a copy of the current well permit if available. No quality testing or cost estimates are required by this statute.

questions.question_text: A home has solar panels and high-efficiency insulation. Colorado's green disclosure for these features is:
questions.explanation: No Colorado statute requires a disclosure of green or energy-efficiency features. Sellers and brokers may voluntarily highlight such features (industry green-features addenda exist), but there is no state mandate.

questions.question_text: A property uses a cistern to collect rainwater. This water source requires:
questions.explanation: Every residential sale must disclose its source of potable water under C.R.S. 38-35.7-104. A source that is neither a well nor a water provider — such as a cistern or spring — is described under the statute's third option.

questions.question_text: Which property type triggers the source of water disclosure requirement?
questions.correct_answer: A home served by a water district
questions.explanation: The source of water disclosure applies to every residential sale contract in Colorado regardless of source (C.R.S. 38-35.7-104) — homes on water districts, municipal systems, wells, springs, and cisterns all disclose their source. It is not limited to non-municipal or problem properties.
